coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive and real-time detection of sialic acid using a solution-gated graphene transistor functionalized with carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Huibin
Yu, Haiyang
Deng, Minghua
Ren, Zhanpeng
Li, Ziqin
Zhang, Lei
Li, Jiashen
Wang, Erjing
Wang, Xianbao
Li, Jinhua
coredata.dc:description: Sialic acid (SA) involves multiple different physiological and pathological processes. The SA level in serum is a reliable biomarker for the early diagnosis of malignant tumors. In this study, a new type of solution-gated graphene transistor was designed and fabricated for highly sensitive and real-time detection of SA by rapid binding of boric acid groups to diol monosaccharides. The gate electrode was modified with synthesized carbon quantum dots having boronic acid on the surface as the specific recognition site of SA. The detection of SA is achieved via the change of the gate surface potential after the conjugation of SA with carbon quantum dots. The device sensitivity can reach 10−19 M and a good linear relationship was present between the electrical current response and logarithmic values of the SA concentrations in the range of 10−19 M to 10−5 M. This sensor also exhibited good selectivity and good stability. Furthermore, the detection of SA in simulated serum samples was also carried out and satisfactory results were acquired, showing the high potential of the prepared sensor in clinical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biosensors
Solution-gated graphene transistors
Sialic acid
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrical characterizations of the gate electrode and SGGT
Detection of simulated serum samples containing sialic acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Structural diagram of the SGGT sensor, and (b) the process chart for modification of the gate electrode. (c) TEM image of CQDs. Inset, the HRTEM image of CQDs. (d) FTIR spectrum of CQDs.

(a) EIS curves of the bare gold electrode (red curve), the CQD-modified electrode (blue curve) and the gate electrode with the binding of SA (green curve). (b) CV curves (Scan rate: 50 mV s−1) of the bare gold electrode (red curve), the CQD-modified electrode (blue curve) and the gate electrode with the binding of SA (green curve). (c) Transfer curves of devices before and after SA binding on the gate electrode. (d) Stability of the device with the CQD modification over time. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The transfer curves of the device at the different concentrations of SA. (b) The ΔV Dirac versus the Logarithmic values of the SA concentrations. (c) Schematic diagram of the potential distribution near the interface of the gate electrode and the electrolyte. (d) The change of potential between two interfaces after adding SA.

(a) The channel current response of the sensor with the different concentrations of SA at V DS = 0.1 V and V G = 0.3 V. (b) The relationship of ΔI DS and logarithmic of SA concentration. (c) The time–current response of the device at the SA concentrations of 1 fM and 100 fM. with V DS = 0.1 V and V G = 0.3 V.

(a) Transfer curves of the device with the CQD probes for the serum sample and different concentrations of SA simulated serum samples. Transfer curves of the device without the CQD probes for the different concentrations of (b) SA in serum and (c) SA in PBS solution. (d) The ΔV Dirac of the device with/without the CQD probes versus the concentration of SA in PBS and SA in serum samples.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive and real-time detection of sialic acid using a solution-gated graphene transistor functionalized with carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials Co-constructed by the Province and Ministry, Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials Co-constructed by the Province and Ministry
Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials
Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials Co-constructed by the Province and Ministry, Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials, The University of Manchester, Manchester M13 9PL, UK
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials, The University of Manchester, Manchester M13 9PL, UK

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sialic acid (SA) involves multiple different physiological and pathological processes. The SA level in serum is a reliable biomarker for the early diagnosis of malignant tumors. In this study, a new type of solution-gated graphene transistor was designed and fabricated for highly sensitive and real-time detection of SA by rapid binding of boric acid groups to diol monosaccharides. The gate electrode was modified with synthesized carbon quantum dots having boronic acid on the surface as the specific recognition site of SA. The detection of SA is achieved via the change of the gate surface potential after the conjugation of SA with carbon quantum dots. The device sensitivity can reach 10−19 M and a good linear relationship was present between the electrical current response and logarithmic values of the SA concentrations in the range of 10−19 M to 10−5 M. This sensor also exhibited good selectivity and good stability. Furthermore, the detection of SA in simulated serum samples was also carried out and satisfactory results were acquired, showing the high potential of the prepared sensor in clinical application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sialic acid (SA) is one kind of negatively charged monosaccharide that exists at the ends of glycoproteins and glycolipids on the cell surface [1–4]. Through the interaction of carbohydrates and proteins, SA involves in multiple physiological and pathological processes, including cell development and recognition, tumor growth, and metastasis [5]. Studies showed that SA could act as a biomarker of cancer owing to its abnormal expression on tumor cells or in serum during the cancer metastasis. The abnormal expression would result in tumor cell maintenance and deterioration, and inhibit the cancer cells apoptosis, thus affecting later medical treatment [6,7]. In different types of cancers such as ovarian cancer [8], lung cancer [9], and colorectal cancer [10], the levels of SA would be increased. More importantly, SA might also play roles in diminishing immune activation, promoting immune escape [11], and enhancing tumor proliferation [12]. Therefore, the detection of SA level in serum is a reliable reference value for the early diagnosis of cancer. The conventional detection methods for SA include colorimetry [13,14], spectrofluorimetry [15,16], chromatography [17] and electrochemistry [18]. However, these methods usually require complex sample pretreatment and complicated equipment, which will lead to high cost and longtime consumption. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a new method for the highly sensitive, rapid and convenient detection of SA.

Compared with traditional detection methods, organic electrochemical transistors (OECTs) have been attracting more attention because of their low device cost, easy fabrication, and easy operation (working voltages less than 1 V) [19]. H. S. Yao et al. [20] reported an OECT biosensor with 3-aminophenylboronic acid-modified gate electrode for SA detection. The detection limit of SA was 8 μM and the response was linear to 2.0 mM. H. X. Ju et al. [21] also reported an OECT biosensor with a similar structure for SA detection. The detection range was from 0.1 mM to 7 mM. Recently, D. Wang et al. [22] used the fibers of PEDOT:PSS/carbon nanotube composite as the channel material and 3-aminophenylboronic acid-modified gate electrode to construct a wearable OECT for SA detection. The sensor exhibited a good linear response range of 1 nM–1 mM. However, the sensitivity of these sensors needs to be improved. A semiconducting material with higher mobility would be preferable to enlarge the electrical current. Graphene, as a typical carbon nanomaterial, has a large conjugated planar structure with higher carrier-transporting capability. Therefore, solution-gated graphene transistors (SGGTs) with the graphene conducting channel would be a better alternative to obtain better performances for the biosensing of SA. At present, SGGTs have been widely developed and utilized in chemosensors and biosensors for the detection of ions [23–25], proteinases [26], DNA [27,28], etc.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, are non-toxic, biocompatible, and easy functionalization for further interaction [29], and are widely used as identification linkers due to the multifarious functional groups on their surface. It has demonstrated that 3-aminophenylboronic acid (3-APBA) can bind to the diol side chains of monosaccharides [30]. Therefore, we use the flexible in sources, easy doping [31] and environmentally friendly [32] hydrothermal synthesis method to synthesize CQDs. However, previously reported detection of SA was all based on the fluorescence properties of CQDs. The sensitivity of SA detection is limited. Electrical detection is another strategy for realizing high sensitivity and accuracy. Herein, based on the advantages of SGGT and CQDs, CQDs-modified SGGT will provide a new convenient method for SA detection.

In this work, a highly sensitive and real-time biosensor of sialic acid based the solution-gated graphene transistor was successfully constructed. The limit of detection reached 10−19 M, demonstrating better sensitivity than that of past report methods and a good linear relationship between the electrical response and logarithmic values of the SA concentrations in the range of 10−19 M to 10−5 M. The sensor also exhibited good selectivity in the presence of different interferences. More importantly, the prepared sensor also realized the detection of SA in the simulated serum samples.

Fabrication of SGGT sensor: A glass substrate was ultrasonically cleaned with acetone, ethanol, and deionized water, respectively, and then placed in an oven for drying. Cr (10 nm) and Au (30 nm) as source/drain and gate electrodes were evaporated onto glass plates by thermal evaporation and then the prepared device treated with O2 plasma to increase the hydrophilicity of the electrodes thus graphene was transferred to the channel. Finally, PDMS well is made as the testing reaction vessel. The structure of the fabricated device was shown in Fig. 1 (a).

Probe immobilization on the gate electrode : Before the immobilization process, the electrode is cleaned with piranha solution (H2SO4: H2O2 = 7:3) to remove impurities on the surface of the electrode and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of the surface of gate electrode is shown in Fig. S1(a). It can be observed that the gold grains on the gate electrode are closely arranged indicate that the gold electrode surface is very clean, which is convenient to the immobilization of the probe. Then the specific immobilization process is shown in Fig. 1(b). A solution of MPA 10 µL (200 mM) was dropped onto the gate electrode and reacted at room temperature for 12 h to carboxylate the gate electrode. Next, 10 µL EDC (20 mM) and NHS (20 mM) were dropped onto the gate electrode to activate the carboxyl groups for 3 h. The SEM image of gate electrode modified by MPA is shown in Fig. S1(b). Compared with Fig. S1(a), the surface of the modified gold electrode becomes more compact. Subsequently, the CQD suspension solution was dropped onto the gate electrode to react for 4 h and the CQDs with the amino groups and hydroxyl radical were immobilized on the MPA surface by the reaction with the carboxyl groups of MPA.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of synthesized CQDs were characterized as shown in Fig. 1(c). The CQDs exhibit good dispersion and their diameters are 4–8 nm. The inset of Fig. 1(c) shows the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of CQDs. The CQDs exhibit clear lattice fringes with a lattice space of 0.23 nm which is correspond to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon. It indicates that the CQDs have good crystallinity. In addition, the PL spectrum was measured to confirm that CQDs were well synthesized, as shown in Fig. S2. Under the excitation of light with a wavelength of 330 nm, the maximum emission wavelength of CQDs is 500 nm. To determine the functional groups on the surface of carbon CQDs, their Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum were measured, as shown in Fig. 1(d). The characteristic absorption peaks at 3445 cm−1, 1618 cm−1, and 1382 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of O–H, CO, and C–N, respectively. Two N–H stretching vibration peaks also observed at 3390 and 3225 cm−1. It indicates that the CQDs contain the –NH2 groups and –OH groups on their surfaces which react with the –COOH groups on the MPA surface and form the covalent bond to be well immobilized on the MPA. In addition, clear absorption peaks at 1150 and 1073 cm−1 can be attributed to the bending vibration of B–O–H and the deformation vibration of B–O–H, respectively. It indicates the boronic acid group has been successfully modified to the CQDs. Boronic acid groups can well recognize the SA targets, which is agreed with our experiment design.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrical characterizations of the gate electrode and SGGT
ce:section.ce:para: To characterize the electrical properties of the gate electrode after the CQD modification and the recognition of SA, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) properties of the gate electrode were measured in the PBS buffer solution with Fe(CN)6 3–/Fe(CN)6 4– (5 mmol/L). Fig. 2 (a) showed the EIS of the bare gold electrode (red curve), the CQD-modified electrode (blue curve) and the gate electrode with the binding of SA (green curve). The EIS spectrum of the bare gold electrode in the high-frequency region is almost a straight line due to good electrical conductivity. After the CQD probe is immobilization on the gate electrode, the surface resistance of the electrode increases due to the insulating property of surface self-assembled films compared with the gold electrode. When the SA was bound by CQDs, due to the negative charge of SA, it repulses negative charges in electrochemical test buffers and increase the impedance. Fig. 2(b) shows the CV curves of the three electrodes. Similarly, when the CQDs were modified on the gate electrode, the redox peak decreased. In further, when the SA was bound to the CQD probe on the gate electrode, the redox peak continued to decrease. The results of ESI and CV indicate that the CQDs are successfully immobilized on the gate electrode and can recognize SA.

To determine the effect of CQD immobilization and the SA binding on the gate electrode, the transfer curves of the device were measured before and after the SA binding on the gate electrode, as shown in Fig. 2(c). When the SA is bound to the CQD probe on the gate electrode, the transfer curve of the device is shifted to the right by 40 mv. When the SA bound with CQDs on the gate electrode, it is equivalent to applying a negative potential to the gate electrode. Therefore, the transfer curve of the device will shift to a higher gate voltage direction. Fig. 2(d) shows the stability of the device in the PBS buffer solution. The Dirac point shift of the device is not obviously observed in 5 h which indicates that the device has good stability in the testing environment and it can be used to detect the SA.

To determine the quantitative relationship between the Dirac point shift and concentration of the SA, the different concentrations of SA were added into the PBS electrolyte and reacted for 10 min to obtain the transfer characteristic. Fig. 3 (a) shows the transfer curves of the device at the concentration of SA in the range of 10−19 M to 10−5 M. It can be found that the transfer curves of the device shift towards the direction of the positive gate voltage with the increase of SA concentration. To quantitatively analyze the relationship between the transfer curve shift and the concentration of SA, the variation of Dirac point voltage (ΔV Dirac) can be extracted for the transfer curves. Fig. 3(b) shows the relationship of the ΔV Dirac and the Logarithmic values of the SA concentrations and there exists a good linear relationship, the correlation coefficient R 2 reaches 0.99. Compared with past reports, the sensitivity of detection can reach 10−19 M (see the Table S1). Because the content of SA in serum is on the mM order of magnitude [33], the relation of ΔV Dirac and the SA concentration on the mM order of magnitude has also been investigated, as shown in Fig. S3(a). The transfer curves of the device still shift towards the positive gate voltage with the increase of the SA concentration and the sensitivity of the sensor can reach 0.009 mM as shown in Fig. S3(b), the relation curve of the ΔV Dirac and the SA concentration from 0.009 mM to 0.2 mM can be well fitted by the expression of Langmuir adsorption isotherm [34] (see the supporting information).

To verify the function of the CQD probes in the SA detection, the transfer curves of the device without the CQD probes have been measured at the different concentrations of the SA, as shown in Fig. S4. No obvious Dirac point shift of the device is observed with the increase of the SA concentration. It indicates that the CQD probes modified on the gate electrode are the key to SA detection. If the response (ΔV Dirac) of the device without the CQD probe modification to the SA with a different concentration is defined as blank noise (N), the response (ΔV Dirac) of the device with the CQD probe modification to the SA with a different concentration can be defined as the signal (S). In general, if the S/N ratio is greater than 3 times [35], the signal can be considered as an effective signal for SA detection. From Fig. 3(a) and Fig. S4, it is calculated that the S/N ratio of the sensors is more than 3 times, implying that the sensors are reliable for SA detection.

Fig. S5 shows the recognition process of the CQD probe with SA. The -BOH groups on the CQD probe will react with the target SA, which results in SA recognition. Fig. 3(c) shows the potential distribution near the interface of the gate electrode and the electrolyte. When SA is bound to the CQD probes, the potential drops because of the electronegativity of SA. As known, when the voltage is applied to the gate of SGGT, the two double electrical layers (EDLs), gate/electrolyte and electrolyte/channel are formed. Fig. 3(d) shows the potential variation of EDLs before and after the SA targets are bound to the CQD probes. The solid line shows the potential distribution before SA targets are bound to the CQD probes. When the SA targets are bound to the CQD probes on the gate electrode, it is equivalent to applying a negative voltage (V offset) at the gate electrode due to the electronegativity of SA targets. Thus, the potential will be redistributed between the two EDLs (dot line). The channel current (I DS) and the effective gate voltage of the SGGT are given by the following Equations [36,37].
(1)
I
DS
≈
W L
μ
C i
V G
eff
-
V
Dirac
-
V
DS
2
·
V DS
when
V DS
⩽
V G
eff
-
V
Dirac
(2)
V
G
eff
=
V G
+
V offset
where W and L are the width and length of the channel, respectively, µ is the mobility of graphene carriers (holes or electrons), Ci is the total capacitance, V G
eff is the effective gate voltage, V DS is the voltage applied to the drain electrode, V G is the voltage applied to the gate electrode, and Voffset is the offset voltage. Because the SA is negatively charged, Voffset is a negative value in Eq. (2), thus to keep V G
eff constant, V G should be increased, which is the reason that the shift of the transfer curve towards the more positive voltage.

And then, the selectivity of the sensor was also tested. The interferences (lactose, D-fructose, D-galactose, and glucose) and the SA were measured at the concentration of 10−5 M. They were in turn dropped into the testing well and their transfer curves were recorded, as shown in Fig. S6(a). The Dirac point shifts of their transfer curves can be extracted, as shown in Fig. S6(b). It can be seen that the value of the Dirac point shift caused by the SA is at least 2.5 times those of other interferences. It indicates that the prepared sensor has good selectivity to SA.

Real-time monitoring reflects the dynamic current changes when the CQDs react with SA on the gate electrode. Fig. 4 (a) shows the channel current response of the device in real-time in different concentrations of SA. The device was measured at V DS = 0.1 V and V G = 0.3 V. As the concentration of SA increase from 10−17 M to 10−9 M, the channel current exhibits a step-like increase. Fig. 4(b) shows the relationship between ΔI DS and the logarithmic SA concentration. ΔI DS and logarithmic of SA concentration fitted by a good linear relationship and the fitting correlation coefficient was 0.978. Fig. 4(c) shows the time–current response of the devices at the SA concentrations of 1 fM and 100 fM. It can be seen that the SA targets completely bound to the CQD probes and the channel current of the device became stable in 24 min. Therefore, the sensors can realize the real-time monitoring of SA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of simulated serum samples containing sialic acid
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical application of the fabricated biosensors, the bovine serum was used to simulate real serum samples. Equal amounts of bovine serum and SA were mixed evenly and the mixture was diluted 1000 times to obtain SA bovine serum samples and then prepared different concentrations of SA bovine serum samples were to test the response of the sensor. Fig. 5 (a) shows the transfer curves of the device with the CQD probes for the different concentrations of SA bovine serum samples tested in PBS solution. It is noted that the Dirac point of the transfer curve shows a slight shift towards the positive gate voltage direction when the bovine serum with 1000 times dilution of PBS solution drops into the testing well. The possible reason is because of nonspecific adsorption on the CQD probe from the serum. It is also observed that the Dirac point of the transfer curve showed the obvious shifts towards the positive gate voltage with the increase of the SA concentration in serum samples from 10−13 M to 10−9 M. It is consistent with pure SA in PBS in Fig. 3(a). At the SA concentration of 10−13 M, the Dirac point shift is far higher than that at pure serum. It implies that components of serum less influence the detection of SA in the serum. To verify the validity of the response signal, devices without probe immobilization were measured at the different concentrations of SA in PBS and SA in serum. Fig. 5(b) and (c) show the transfer curves of the device without the CQD probes for the different concentrations of SA in serum and SA in PBS solution. It is noticed that no obvious shifts of the Dirac point are observed if the CQD probes is not immobilized on the gate electrode. It indicates that the CQD probe is the key to the detection of SA. Fig. 5(d) shows the ΔV Dirac extracted from the transfer curves in Fig. 5 (a), (b) and (c). The response of the device with the CQD probes to the SA defined as the signal (S) and the response of the device without the CQD probes to the SA defined as the signal (N). It can be found that the S/N is larger than 3. It indicates the response signal of the sensor to SA is valid. Therefore, the SGGT sensors can realize SA detection in the serum sample.

In summary, a highly sensitive and real-time biosensor for sialic acid based on the solution-gated graphene transistor was successfully fabricated. The carbon quantum dots with the boronic acid groups were synthesized by the hydrothermal method and modified on the gate electrode as the recognition probe of sialic acid. Different from past researches based on the fluorescence detection of CQDs, the CQD-modified SGGT can combine the signal amplification function with the advantages of CQDs. The sensitivity of the obtained sensor can reach 10−19 M and exhibits good linear relationship between the electrical response and logarithmic values of the sialic acid concentrations in the range of 10−19 M to 10−5 M. The sensor also exhibits good selectivity from different interferences. More importantly, the detection of sialic acid in the simulated serum samples was also realized. This study involves better sensitivity and does not require complex operation processing. It provides a new strategy for the early detection of cancers and other related diseases.

Huibin Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Haiyang Yu: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation. Minghua Deng: Data curation, Validation. Zhanpeng Ren: Data curation, Validation. Ziqin Li: Data curation, Validation. Lei Zhang: Data curation, Validation. Jiashen Li: Data curation, Validation. Erjing Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Xianbao Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Jinhua Li: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Hubei Provincial Department of Science & Technology (2020BIB020), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51673060), the Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Chemical Materials and Devices of Ministry of Education, Jianghan University (JDGD-202205) and the Overseas Expertise Introduction Center for Discipline Innovation (D18025).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108676.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
